# Cursor-agent CLI does not register skills from plugins (IDE does ‚Äî parity gap)

https://forum.cursor.com/t/cursor-agent-cli-does-not-register-skills-from-plugins-ide-does-parity-gap/158947

Support Bug Reports

droth_nyt(Dan Roth) April 24, 2026, 3:18pm 1

### Where does the bug appear (feature/product)?

Cursor IDE

### Describe the Bug

## Summary

Skills bundled inside plugins are loaded by the plugin subsystem but never reach the agent‚Äôs skill registry in the headless`cursor-agent` CLI. The IDE agent surfaces plugin skills from the official Cursor Marketplace, but the CLI does not ‚Äî not from Cursor marketplace plugins, not from local plugins (`~/.cursor/plugins/local/`), and not from Claude Code marketplace plugins. Skills only register when placed directly in one of four well-known paths. This breaks the value proposition of plugin-based skill distribution for any workflow that uses the CLI (CI, evals, headless agents).

## Request ID / Privacy note

Our Cursor Enterprise admin policy enforces Privacy Mode, so I cannot enable Share Data to produce a content-visible Request ID. This bug is deterministic and reproducible on any machine via the minimal canary case below ‚Äî the Cursor team should not need session content to reproduce it.

For completeness, these are the`cursor-agent` CLI`session_id` s from my test runs (metadata-level visibility only):

| Behavior | session_id |
| --- | --- |
| Plugin skill not surfaced (Claude Code install) | `c1c57a65-76df-43ec-a50f-9e5da6f63cd6` |
| Skill enumeration ‚Äî plugins absent | `ffad3026-6565-4690-ba98-303cab03b386` |
| Skill fires from`~/.cursor/skills/`(control) | `98dead3b-504b-4f5c-b2d9-21eafe48ba9e` |
| Skill fires via symlink into`~/.cursor/skills/` | `3ffbf1cf-225d-495c-8ada-8c30875acfd3` |

Happy to coordinate through our Enterprise account rep if more detailed evidence is needed ‚Äî please reach out if that‚Äôs the preferred channel for this class of issue.

## What actually happens

The plugin loader logs success, but the agent has zero knowledge of the skill‚Äôs content. When asked to enumerate available skills, the CLI returns only skills from:

- ` /.claude/skills/`
- ` /.cursor/skills/`
- `~/.cursor/skills/`
- `~/.cursor/skills-cursor/`

Plugin skill paths are never returned, regardless of whether the plugin is:

| Source | IDE registers skills? | CLI registers skills? |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Cursor public marketplace (`~/.cursor/plugins/cache/cursor-public/*/skills/`) |
| Cursor local plugin (`~/.cursor/plugins/local/*/skills/`) | (likely yes, untested) |
| Claude Code marketplace (`~/.claude/plugins/cache/*/*/skills/`) |

## Workaround currently in use

Symlinking plugin skill directories into`~/.cursor/skills/`:

```
ln -s \
  ~/.claude/plugins/cache/<marketplace>/<plugin>/<sha>/skills/<skill> \
  ~/.cursor/skills/bridge--<plugin>--<skill>

```

Works because`~/.cursor/skills/` is scanned with plain`readdir` and symlinks are followed. But:

- Plugin update changes the SHA directory, breaking the symlink
- Doesn‚Äôt scale ‚Äî requires a sync script per install/update
- Only surfaces skills; plugin commands, agents, and hooks still don‚Äôt port

## Asks

1. CLI parity with IDE: have`cursor-agent` scan plugin skill paths the same way the IDE does.
2. Claude Code plugin skill surfacing (IDE): the existing`loadClaudePlugin` code path logs ‚Äúloaded‚Äù but the skills don‚Äôt reach the IDE agent‚Äôs skill registry either (separate issue; logs available).
3. Config escape hatch (nice-to-have): an`additionalSkillPaths` field in`cli-config.json` or`~/.cursor/settings.json` for users/orgs that want to add arbitrary skill directories.

## Why this matters

We‚Äôre evaluating Cursor vs Claude Code for org-wide rollout (NYT, Enterprise plan). Today, the same plugin that provides working skills to`claude` users contributes nothing to`cursor-agent` users ‚Äî and even Cursor‚Äôs own plugin system doesn‚Äôt work in headless mode. For any team running evals, CI agents, or a consistent IDE + headless story, this forces a second, parallel distribution mechanism (flat`~/.cursor/skills/` files) just to keep the CLI functional.

## Evidence attachments

Happy to share if useful:

- Full stream-json traces showing skill-enumeration request and grep-search-for-missing-skill behavior (6 NDJSON files)
- `Cursor Plugins.log` excerpts showing`loadClaudePlugin` and`loadFromMarketplaceSource`‚Äúsuccess‚Äù messages for plugins whose skills never register
- Symlink-bridge reproduction showing the same SKILL.md registers when placed at`~/.cursor/skills/` but not at`~/.cursor/plugins/local/*/skills/`

### Steps to Reproduce

## Reproduction ‚Äî minimal local case

Rules out marketplace-specific issues. No external dependencies.

Create`~/.cursor/plugins/local/repro/.cursor-plugin/plugin.json`:

```
{ "name": "repro" }

```

Create`~/.cursor/plugins/local/repro/skills/canary/SKILL.md`:

```
---
name: canary
description: If you can see this skill, the CLI registered a local plugin skill.
---
# Canary skill
Respond with "CANARY-SEEN" when asked anything.

```

Run:

```
cursor-agent -p --mode ask --output-format stream-json \
  "Please list the skills you know about. Do not search the filesystem."

```

1. Observe that the`canary` skill does not appear in the response.
2. Move the skill to`~/.cursor/skills/canary/SKILL.md`(remove the plugin). It now appears. Same file, only path differs.

## Reproduction ‚Äî Cursor public marketplace

Cursor plugin log at`~/Library/Application Support/Cursor/logs/ /window1/exthost/anysphere.cursor-agent-exec/Cursor Plugins.log` shows:

```
loadFromMarketplaceSource: Found cached plugin: <name> at <path>
loadAllPlugins completed ... total=N plugins, failures=0

```

Run:

```
cursor-agent -p --mode ask --output-format stream-json \
  "List every skill registered in your current context."

```

1. Confirm a Cursor marketplace plugin with a skill is installed (e.g.,`dbt`,`glean`,`atlassian`).
2. Observe that plugin skills are not in the list, despite the plugin loading successfully.

### Expected Behavior

A skill bundled in a plugin (Cursor or Claude Code format) that is successfully loaded by the plugin subsystem should be discoverable by the agent, the same way directly-placed SKILL.md files are.

### Operating System

MacOS

### Version Information

## Environment

- Cursor IDE version:`3.1.17`
- cursor-agent CLI:`2026.04.17-479fd04`
- OS: macOS 25.4.0 (darwin/arm64)
- Subscription: Enterprise
- Model tested: Opus 4.6 1M

### Does this stop you from using Cursor

No - Cursor works, but with this issue

Skills not shown in Cursor CLI while visible and working in desktop app

Skills not shown in Cursor CLI while visible and working in desktop app

Skill-indexer ‚Äî and an open question about how AI agent skills should be distributed

Skills not shown in Cursor CLI while visible and working in desktop app

Excessive Token Usage: Cursor auto-loads too many "Skills" from ~/.claude/skills at conversation start

Kaloyan_Yosifov(Kaloyan Yosifov) April 27, 2026, 6:44am 5

I can confirm that the IDE correctly loads plugins from both local sources and Claude. However, the Cursor CLI is failing to do so. Interestingly, this feels like a server-side feature flag issue; I‚Äôm using version 2026.04.17-787b533, and while the CLI recognized the plugins one day, it stopped the next without any configuration changes. I‚Äôve observed the same intermittent behavior in Docker containers. They work initially, then suddenly failed to load plugins.

Since Thursday it cannot seem to even load subagents in the root .cursor folder

Robert_Luo(Robert Luo) April 28, 2026, 4:55am 6

It doesn‚Äôt demonstrate any skills, and the CLI is completely unusable. It was possible before.

Colin(Colin) May 4, 2026, 6:18pm 9

Hey all,

I can confirm that plugins are not currently working in the CLI. We‚Äôll update the docs to reflect this, and we‚Äôre hoping to dedicate some time to this soon!

Kaloyan_Yosifov(Kaloyan Yosifov) May 7, 2026, 7:41am 10

On the new version`v2026.05.05-84a231c` now it works (both claude and local plugins).

Is this still experimental?

Colin(Colin) May 7, 2026, 7:58pm 13

Hi@Kaloyan_Yosifov, we‚Äôve re-enabled the feature flag for plugins in the CLI. Fingers crossed we won‚Äôt need to roll it back! Any feedback you can share on Plugins in the CLI would be greatly appreciated and incredibly valuable to us!

# How to Write and Test Cursor Plugins Locally (The Part the Docs Don‚Äôt Tell You)

Vitek Tajzich

6 min read

Mar 2, 2026

https://medium.com/m/signin?actionUrl=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2F_%2Fvote%2Fp%2F4eee705d7f76&operation=register&redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2F%40v.tajzich%2Fhow-to-write-and-test-cursor-plugins-locally-the-part-the-docs-dont-tell-you-4eee705d7f76&user=Vitek+Tajzich&userId=4726de7c7d9e

--

https://medium.com/m/signin?actionUrl=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2F_%2Frepost%2Fp%2F4eee705d7f76&operation=register&redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2F%40v.tajzich%2Fhow-to-write-and-test-cursor-plugins-locally-the-part-the-docs-dont-tell-you-4eee705d7f76&user=Vitek+Tajzich&userId=4726de7c7d9e

https://medium.com/m/signin?actionUrl=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2F_%2Fbookmark%2Fp%2F4eee705d7f76&operation=register&redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2F%40v.tajzich%2Fhow-to-write-and-test-cursor-plugins-locally-the-part-the-docs-dont-tell-you-4eee705d7f76

Listen

Share

Cursor has a plugin system. The official docs cover the structure well ‚Äî manifest, commands, skills, rules. What they don‚Äôt cover is how to test your plugin locally during development without publishing to the marketplace.

I built a few plugins over the past months and had to figure out the local dev loop myself. The answer lives in how Cursor integrates with Claude Code‚Äôs config directories. The integration itself is a supported path, but the local plugin development workflow around it is barely documented. This article covers that gap.

Note: everything here was tested on macOS (Cursor 2.5.x). The config paths and sqlite commands are macOS-specific. The general approach should transfer to Linux/Windows with adjusted paths, but I haven‚Äôt verified that.

## What‚Äôs a Plugin, Briefly

A plugin is a container. It can bundle primitives such as rules (persistent AI guidance in`.mdc` files), skills (specialized agent capabilities in`SKILL.md` files), commands (markdown-defined actions), hooks, agents, and MCP servers into one distributable package.

The only required file is`.cursor-plugin/plugin.json`:

```
{  "name": "my-plugin",  "version": "1.0.0",  "description": "What it does in one sentence",  "author": { "name": "Your Name" },  "commands": "./commands/",  "skills": "./skills/",  "rules": "./rules/"}
```

One thing worth knowing early: Cursor plugins use`.cursor-plugin/` for the manifest directory. Claude Code plugins use`.claude-plugin/`. Cursor integrates with parts of Claude Code's config surface ‚Äî which is exactly what makes local testing possible. More on that below.

## The Minimal Plugin Structure

Here‚Äôs a real directory tree from one of my plugins:

```
my-plugin/‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ .cursor-plugin/‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ plugin.json‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ commands/‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ do-something.md‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ do-another-thing.md‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ install-plugin.md‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ rules/‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ safety.mdc‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ skills/‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ my-skill/‚îÇ       ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ SKILL.md‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ scripts/    ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ install-plugin.sh    ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ lib.sh
```

Commands are markdown files with YAML frontmatter. The agent reads them and follows the steps:

```
---name: my-commanddescription: What the command does---
```

```
# My Command## Steps1. Ask the user for the thing.2. Run `bash <plugin_root>/scripts/do-the-thing.sh --name <name>`.3. Report the result.
```

Rules are`.mdc` files with frontmatter controlling when they apply (`alwaysApply: true`, or scoped via`globs`). Skills are`SKILL.md` files in named subdirectories ‚Äî the agent invokes them based on task context.

If you don‚Äôt specify paths in`plugin.json`, Cursor auto-discovers components from the default directory names. Explicit paths override auto-discovery.

## The Local Testing Problem

The docs have a submission checklist that says ‚ÄúPlugin has been tested locally.‚Äù Great advice. How?

Claude Code CLI has`--plugin-dir ./my-plugin` for this. You point it at your plugin directory and it loads everything. Clean.

Cursor IDE doesn‚Äôt have that flag. The Marketplace is the intended distribution channel, and there‚Äôs no built-in ‚Äúload from local directory‚Äù option in the IDE. So if you‚Äôre developing a Cursor plugin and want to see your commands show up in the agent‚Äôs palette, you need to register it manually.

## The .claude Directory

Here‚Äôs what I found: Cursor‚Äôs agent reads plugin registration from`~/.claude/`‚Äî it shares Claude Code's config surface. The integration is a known supported path (Cursor's third-party hooks docs reference it), but the local plugin dev workflow built on top of it isn't spelled out anywhere.

You need three things, plus possibly a fourth depending on your Cursor version.

## Step 1: Copy your plugin files

What worked for me: put your plugin into`~/.cursor/plugins/ /`:

```
PLUGIN_NAME="my-plugin"TARGET="$HOME/.cursor/plugins/$PLUGIN_NAME"rm -rf "$TARGET"mkdir -p "$TARGET"for dir in .cursor-plugin commands rules skills scripts; do  [ -d "$dir" ] && cp -R "$dir" "$TARGET/"done
```

## Step 2: Register in ~/.claude/plugins/installed_plugins.json

In my testing, this is the file Cursor picks up to discover locally-installed plugins. Create it (or merge into it) with your plugin entry:

```
{  "plugins": {    "my-plugin@local": [      {        "scope": "user",        "installPath": "/Users/you/.cursor/plugins/my-plugin"      }    ]  }}
```

The`@local` suffix appears to signal a locally-installed plugin, as opposed to one pulled from the marketplace. Use an absolute path for`installPath`‚Äî relative paths didn't work reliably in my testing.

## Step 3: Enable in ~/.claude/settings.json

Registration alone doesn‚Äôt activate the plugin. You also need to flip the enable flag:

```
{  "enabledPlugins": {    "my-plugin@local": true  }}
```

## Step 4 (if needed): Enable third-party content import

Depending on your Cursor version, third-party plugin loading may be gated behind a setting. Look for ‚ÄúInclude third-party Plugins, Skills, and other configs‚Äù under Settings > Features (older builds may call it ‚ÄúThird-party skills‚Äù). If it‚Äôs off, flip it on.

## Get Vitek Tajzich‚Äôs stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.

Subscribe

Subscribe

Remember me for faster sign in

If the UI toggle doesn‚Äôt exist in your build, the sqlite fallback worked for me on macOS with Cursor 2.5.x. Close Cursor first, then:

```
DB="$HOME/Library/Application Support/Cursor/User/globalStorage/state.vscdb"[ -f "$DB" ] && sqlite3 "$DB" \  "INSERT OR REPLACE INTO ItemTable (key, value)   VALUES ('cursor/thirdPartyExtensibilityEnabled', 'true');"
```

This key may change in future builds. If the DB doesn‚Äôt exist or the write fails, skip this step and check the UI settings instead.

After these steps: restart Cursor. Your commands should appear in the agent‚Äôs command palette.

## Automate It

Doing this manually on every change is tedious. I wrap it in a single`install-plugin.sh` that lives in the plugin's`scripts/` directory.

The tricky part is steps 2 and 3 ‚Äî you need to upsert into JSON files that may already contain entries from other plugins. Don‚Äôt just overwrite them. I use inline Python for this because`jq` is not always available and Python ships with most macOS setups (verify with`python3 --version`):

```
#!/usr/bin/env bashset -euo pipefailcommand -v python3 >/dev/null || { echo "python3 required"; exit 1; }
```

```
SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"REPO_ROOT="$(cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/.." && pwd)"PLUGIN_NAME="my-plugin"PLUGIN_ID="${PLUGIN_NAME}@local"TARGET="$HOME/.cursor/plugins/$PLUGIN_NAME"CLAUDE_PLUGINS="$HOME/.claude/plugins/installed_plugins.json"CLAUDE_SETTINGS="$HOME/.claude/settings.json"# 1. Copy plugin filesrm -rf "$TARGET"mkdir -p "$TARGET" "$HOME/.claude/plugins"for dir in .cursor-plugin commands rules skills scripts; do  [[ -d "$REPO_ROOT/$dir" ]] && cp -R "$REPO_ROOT/$dir" "$TARGET/"done# 2. Register in installed_plugins.json (upsert, don't clobber)python3 - "$CLAUDE_PLUGINS" "$PLUGIN_ID" "$TARGET" <<'PY'import json, os, syspath, pid, ipath = sys.argv[1], sys.argv[2], sys.argv[3]data = {}if os.path.exists(path):    try: data = json.load(open(path))    except: data = {}plugins = data.get("plugins", {})entries = [e for e in plugins.get(pid, [])           if not (isinstance(e, dict) and e.get("scope") == "user")]entries.insert(0, {"scope": "user", "installPath": ipath})plugins[pid] = entriesdata["plugins"] = pluginsos.makedirs(os.path.dirname(path), exist_ok=True)json.dump(data, open(path, "w"), indent=2)PY# 3. Enable in settings.json (upsert, don't clobber)python3 - "$CLAUDE_SETTINGS" "$PLUGIN_ID" <<'PY'import json, os, syspath, pid = sys.argv[1], sys.argv[2]data = {}if os.path.exists(path):    try: data = json.load(open(path))    except: data = {}data.setdefault("enabledPlugins", {})[pid] = Trueos.makedirs(os.path.dirname(path), exist_ok=True)json.dump(data, open(path, "w"), indent=2)PYecho "Installed. Restart Cursor."
```

The install script itself can be exposed as a plugin command. I have an`install-plugin.md` command that tells the agent to run`bash <plugin_root>/scripts/install-plugin.sh`. First install is manual, every install after that is a command.

## The Dev Loop

It‚Äôs simple and it‚Äôs not pretty:

1. Edit your plugin sources in your repo.
2. Run`bash scripts/install-plugin.sh`.
3. Restart Cursor (Cmd+Shift+P ‚Üí ‚ÄúReload Window‚Äù sometimes works, full restart is safer).
4. Test your commands, skills, and rules in the agent.
5. Repeat.

There‚Äôs no hot-reload. No watch mode. No incremental updates. You copy files, you restart, you test. It‚Äôs the kind of workflow that makes you appreciate how spoiled we are with frontend dev servers.

But it works. And right now, it‚Äôs the only way I‚Äôve found to iterate on Cursor plugins without publishing to the marketplace.

## What I Wish I Knew Sooner

A few things that cost me time:

`.cursor-plugin/` vs`.claude-plugin/`‚Äî Cursor uses the former. Claude Code uses the latter. If you're looking at Claude Code plugin docs and copying the structure, you'll create`.claude-plugin/` and wonder why Cursor doesn't pick it up.

Don‚Äôt install under`~/.cursor/skills/` only. I tried this first. Skills get loaded, but commands don't register. You need the full`~/.cursor/plugins/ /` path plus the`.claude/` registration.

Check both JSON files. I‚Äôve hit a case where a plugin showed as installed but didn‚Äôt load ‚Äî`installed_plugins.json` had the entry but`enabledPlugins` in`settings.json` didn't. Always verify both files after install. This may be version-specific, but it cost me an hour the first time.

Restart means restart. Reload Window works sometimes. When it doesn‚Äôt and you‚Äôre staring at a command palette that doesn‚Äôt show your new commands, just quit and reopen Cursor. Save yourself the debugging.

The plugin system is young. This hack will probably become unnecessary once Cursor adds proper local dev tooling ‚Äî something like`--plugin-dir` but for the IDE, not just the CLI. Until then, the`~/.claude/` directory is your friend.

results.text:
# Issue: dotnet/skills #523

- Repository: dotnet/skills | Repository for skills to assist AI coding agents with .NET and C# | 3K stars | C#

## Consider releasing a plugin for Cursor users

- Author: [@scharnyw](https://github.com/scharnyw)
- State: closed (completed)
- Labels: meta
- Created: 2026-04-14T02:13:04Z
- Updated: 2026-05-01T21:46:31Z
- Closed: 2026-04-20T12:51:27Z
- Closed by: [@Evangelink](https://github.com/Evangelink)

Similar to #517, but for Cursor.

Considering that Cursor is a highly-popular agentic coding tool (second highest market share after Claude Code, as some reports show), and that it has both an official marketplace and support for local marketplaces (imported from GitHub), is it in the plans to release a Cursor plugin to make usage easier for Cursor users?

Cursor does support `marketplace.json`, so I guess the only change necessary is to place the `marketplace.json` under `/.cursor-plugin`. It would be an additional plus if the plugins can be released via the official Cursor marketplace (requires manual submission and review by Cursor team).

References:
https://cursor.com/docs/plugins
https://cursor.com/docs/reference/plugins

---

### Timeline

**@Evangelink** commented ¬∑ Apr 17, 2026 at 6pm

> @danmoseley @timheuer WDYT?

**@danmoseley** commented ¬∑ Apr 17, 2026 at 7:47pm

> I would assume anything we have here for claude we're happy to do for other major tools. @ManishJayaswal are we open to a PR here

**danmoseley** added label `meta` ¬∑ Apr 17, 2026 at 7:48pm

**@timheuer** commented ¬∑ Apr 18, 2026 at 3:07pm

> Can we just add a symlink in that location?

**@scharnyw** commented ¬∑ Apr 20, 2026 at 6:50am ¬∑ Author

> > Can we just add a symlink in that location?
> 
> Yes, but we need a `marketplace.json` under `/.cursor-plugin`. I can submit a PR if it's OK.
> 
> Also the latest Cursor version doesn't seem to support symlink in local plugin directory, but I guess that's a bug that should be addressed by Cursor.

**Evangelink** mentioned this in PR [#549: Add Cursor plugin marketplace support](https://github.com/dotnet/skills/pull/549) ¬∑ Apr 20, 2026 at 9:46am

**@Evangelink** commented ¬∑ Apr 20, 2026 at 9:50am

> @scharnyw would you be able to validate the change? I don't have cursor.

**@ViktorHofer** commented ¬∑ Apr 20, 2026 at 10:33am

> > Can we just add a symlink in that location?
> 
> There's already a PR in the making that doesn't use a symlink but I just wanted to underline that we can't use symlinks. We did that initially and it broke customers on Windows machines that didn't have the Developer Modes enabled in the OS settings.

**@scharnyw** commented ¬∑ Apr 20, 2026 at 11:19am ¬∑ Author

> > > Can we just add a symlink in that location?
> > 
> > There's already a PR in the making that doesn't use a symlink but I just wanted to underline that we can't use symlinks. We did that initially and it broke customers on Windows machines that didn't have the Developer Modes enabled in the OS settings.
> 
> Sorry I misunderstood the original comment. I thought you meant a symlink from the Cursor local plugin directory to a cloned version of the repo.
> 
> But yes, symlinks within a Git repo have various issues on Windows. So I guess a more cross-platform solution would be to manually keeping the `marketplace.json` files in sync.

**Evangelink** closed this ¬∑ Apr 20, 2026 at 12:51pm

**@KoshelevS** commented ¬∑ May 1, 2026 at 9:46pm

> The Cursor IDE doesn't include the `dotnet-skills` plugins in the marketplace, so I tried using them locally. Copying the entire `dotnet-agent-skills` folder didn't work, but I could see them in the Cursor IDE after these steps:
> 
> 1. Copy the necessary plugin folders to `$env:USERPROFILE\.cursor\plugins\local`
> 2. Create a `./cursor-plugin/plugin.json` file in each plugin folder with basic information, like:
> 
> ```json
> {
> "name": "dotnet",
> "description": "Collection of core .NET skills for handling common .NET coding tasks."
> }
> ```

results.title: Configure AI Assistants - Atmos
results.text:
Configure AI Assistants | atmos

On this page

Configure your AI coding assistant to use Atmos agent skills. Skills give the AI deep knowledge of Atmos conventions, stack configuration, Terraform orchestration, and more. Each tool has its own installation method.

## You will learn

- Claude Code: install via the Cloud Posse plugin marketplace
- Cursor, Windsurf, GitHub Copilot: reference`AGENTS.md` from the Atmos repo
- Gemini CLI, OpenAI Codex: symlink or copy the`agent-skills/` directory
- Skills activate automatically -- no special syntax needed

## Install Atmos Skills‚Äã

Atmos users install the`atmos` binary -- you do not need to clone the Atmos repository. Skills are installed separately through your AI tool's own mechanism.

### Claude Code Plugin (Recommended)‚Äã

The fastest way to add Atmos skills is via the Cloud Posse plugin marketplace. No files to copy -- skills are installed with two commands and persist across sessions:

```bash
# One-time marketplace setup/plugin marketplace add cloudposse/atmos# Install the Atmos skills plugin/plugin install atmos@cloudposse
```

To auto-configure skills for your entire team, add marketplace and plugin settings to your project's`.claude/settings.json`(see Team Auto-Discovery below).

### Other AI Tools‚Äã

Gemini CLI, OpenAI Codex, Cursor, Windsurf, and GitHub Copilot do not have third-party marketplace support for Atmos skills. For these tools, use Atmos vendoring to pull the skills into your project.

Add the following source to your`vendor.yaml`:

```yaml
apiVersion: atmos/v1kind: AtmosVendorConfigmetadata:  name: atmos-agent-skills  description: Vendor Atmos AI agent skillsspec:  sources:    - component: "agent-skills"      source: "github.com/cloudposse/atmos.git//agent-skills?ref={{.Version}}"      version: "main"      targets:        - "agent-skills"
```

Then pull the skills:

```bash
atmos vendor pull --component agent-skills
```

This downloads the`agent-skills/` directory into your project with the correct structure intact. To update skills later, run the same command again.

Then configure your AI tool using the instructions below.

## Tool-Specific Configuration‚Äã

- Claude Code
- OpenAI Codex
- Gemini CLI
- Cursor
- Windsurf
- GitHub Copilot
- Other Tools

Config directory:`.claude/`| Instruction file:`CLAUDE.md`| Skills:`.claude/skills/` or via plugins

The recommended way to install Atmos skills in Claude Code is via the Cloud Posse plugin marketplace.

#### Plugin Installation (Recommended)‚Äã

```bash
# Step 1: Add the Cloud Posse marketplace (one-time setup)/plugin marketplace add cloudposse/atmos# Step 2: Install the Atmos skills plugin (all 21 skills)/plugin install atmos@cloudposse
```

Skills are available immediately after installation and persist across sessions. One plugin installs all 21 skills -- the AI loads only the relevant skill on demand, so there is no performance overhead.

How it works: The`cloudposse/atmos` GitHub repository serves as the marketplace. When you add it, Claude Code fetches`.claude-plugin/marketplace.json` directly from the repo via the GitHub API. When you install the plugin, Claude Code downloads the skills directory and caches it locally. No central registry or approval process is involved -- the GitHub repo IS the marketplace.

#### Uninstalling‚Äã

```bash
# Remove the plugin/plugin uninstall atmos@cloudposse# Remove the marketplace (optional)/plugin marketplace remove cloudposse
```

#### Team Auto-Discovery‚Äã

Teams can auto-configure the marketplace for all team members by adding to the project's`.claude/settings.json`:

```json
{  "extraKnownMarketplaces": {    "cloudposse": {      "source": {        "source": "github",        "repo": "cloudposse/atmos"      }    }  },  "enabledPlugins": {    "atmos@cloudposse": true  }}
```

When team members trust the repository folder, Claude Code prompts them to install these plugins automatically.

#### Official Anthropic Marketplace (Alternative)‚Äã

Atmos plugins may also be available in the official Anthropic marketplace. If listed there, you can install without adding a marketplace first:

```bash
/plugin install atmos@claude-plugin-directory
```

Check the "Discover" tab in`/plugin` to see if Atmos plugins are available in the official directory.

#### Auto-Discovery (Atmos Contributors)‚Äã

When working directly in the Atmos repo, Claude Code automatically discovers skills via the`.claude/skills` symlinks -- no plugin installation needed.

```bash
# Skills are auto-discovered in the Atmos repoclaude# Or add the skills directory from another locationclaude --add-dir /path/to/atmos/agent-skills
```

Claude Code loads`AGENTS.md` as the router, then activates the relevant`SKILL.md` based on your question.

## How Skills Are Activated‚Äã

You do not invoke skills manually. Your AI tool activates the right skill automatically based on your question:

1. At session start, the AI loads lightweight metadata (name and description) from each installed skill
2. When you ask a question about Atmos, the AI matches your question to the most relevant skill's description
3. The full skill content loads on demand, giving the AI accurate, up-to-date guidance
4. Reference files load only when the AI needs deeper detail (e.g., full command reference, schema examples)

For example, if you ask "How do I configure stack imports?", the AI automatically activates the`atmos-stacks` skill and uses its content to answer correctly -- no special syntax or commands needed.

tip

For Claude Code plugin installations, activation uses the`description` field in each SKILL.md's frontmatter. For file-based installations (Codex, Gemini, Windsurf, Copilot), the`AGENTS.md` router maps your task to the right skill via its skill index table. Both mechanisms are automatic.

## Related‚Äã

- AI Agent Skills-- Full list of available skills, folder structure, and SKILL.md format
- Setup Editor (IDE)-- Configure VS Code and DevContainers for Atmos

Install Atmos Skills

- Claude Code Plugin (Recommended)
- Other AI Tools

results.title: Plugins: Discover and Install | The AI Agent Factory - Panaversity
results.text:
Plugins: Discover and Install | Agent Factory

On this page

Teach Me

Sign in to access Teach Me mode

Ask

Sign in to ask questions

Copy as MarkdownCtrl+‚áß+C

Updated Mar 16, 2026

You've learned to create skills, configure hooks, and use subagents. But what if someone has already built exactly what you need?

---

## What Are Plugins?‚Äã

A plugin bundles multiple Claude Code components into one installable package:

| Component | What It Does |
| --- | --- |
| Skills | Autonomous capabilities Claude discovers and uses |
| Commands | Slash commands like`/commit-commands:commit` |
| Agents | Specialized subagents for focused tasks |
| Hooks | Event automation (format on save, validate on edit) |
| MCP servers | External integrations (GitHub, Slack, etc.) |

Think of plugins as: Complete capability packages. Instead of manually setting up skills, hooks, agents, and MCP servers separately, you install one plugin and everything works together.

---

## Why Use Plugins?‚Äã

Without plugins, adding GitHub integration means:

1. Test everything works together
2. Maybe add hooks for automation
3. Maybe create skills to use it well
4. Configure it in your settings
5. Find an MCP server for GitHub

With plugins, you run:

```text
/plugin install github@claude-plugins-official
```

Done. GitHub integration works‚Äîincluding MCP config, any bundled skills, and automation hooks.

The principle: Check what exists before building from scratch.

---

## You Already Have a Plugin Marketplace‚Äã

Run this command in Claude Code right now:

```text
/plugin
```

What you'll see:

```text
‚îÇ Plugin Manager                                                                       ‚îÇ‚îÇ                                                                                      ‚îÇ‚îÇ   Discover   ‚îÇ   Installed   ‚îÇ   Marketplaces   ‚îÇ   Errors                          ‚îÇ‚îÇ                                                                                      ‚îÇ‚îÇ   Code intelligence                                                                  ‚îÇ‚îÇ   ‚ùØ typescript-lsp - TypeScript/JavaScript language server                          ‚îÇ‚îÇ     python-lsp - Python language server (Pyright)                                   ‚îÇ‚îÇ     rust-analyzer-lsp - Rust language server                                        ‚îÇ‚îÇ     gopls-lsp - Go language server                                                  ‚îÇ‚îÇ                                                                                      ‚îÇ‚îÇ   External integrations                                                              ‚îÇ‚îÇ     github - GitHub integration                                                      ‚îÇ‚îÇ     slack - Slack integration                                                        ‚îÇ‚îÇ     linear - Linear project management                                               ‚îÇ‚îÇ                                                                                      ‚îÇ‚îÇ   Development workflows                                                              ‚îÇ‚îÇ     commit-commands - Git commit workflows                                           ‚îÇ‚îÇ     pr-review-toolkit - Pull request review agents                                   ‚îÇ
```

The official Anthropic marketplace is automatically available. No setup needed.

Use Tab to switch between tabs:

- Errors: Debug plugin issues
- Marketplaces: Manage plugin sources
- Installed: See what you've installed
- Discover: Browse available plugins

---

## Try It Now: Install Your First Plugin‚Äã

Let's install commit-commands‚Äîa plugin that helps with git workflows.

### Option 1: Use the UI‚Äã

1. Choose User scope (available in all projects)
2. Press Enter to see details
3. Find commit-commands under "Development workflows"
4. Go to the Discover tab
5. Run`/plugin`

### Option 2: Install Directly‚Äã

```text
/plugin install commit-commands@claude-plugins-official
```

What happens:

- Plugin appears in your Installed tab
- New commands become available immediately
- Plugin downloads and installs

---

## Try It Now: Use Your New Plugin‚Äã

After installing commit-commands, make a small change to any file, then run:

```text
/commit-commands:commit
```

What happens:

1. Creates the commit
2. Generates a commit message based on the diff
3. Plugin stages your changes

That's it! You just extended Claude Code with one command.

---

## What's in the Official Marketplace?‚Äã

| Category | Plugins | What They Do |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Code intelligence | `typescript-lsp`,`python-lsp`,`rust-analyzer-lsp`,`gopls-lsp` | Jump to definitions, find references, see type errors |
| External integrations | `github`,`gitlab`,`slack`,`linear`,`notion`,`figma` | Connect to external services |
| Development workflows | `commit-commands`,`pr-review-toolkit`,`plugin-dev` | Git workflows, PR reviews, plugin creation |
| Output styles | `explanatory-output-style`,`learning-output-style` | Customize how Claude responds |

### Code Intelligence Plugins‚Äã

These use the Language Server Protocol(LSP)‚Äîthe same technology that powers VS Code's code intelligence.

After installing (e.g.,`typescript-lsp`), Claude can:

- See type errors immediately after edits
- Find all references to a variable
- Jump to function definitions

Note: LSP plugins require the language server binary installed on your system. If you see "Executable not found," install the required binary:

| Plugin | Binary Required |
| --- | --- |
| `typescript-lsp` | `typescript-language-server` |
| `python-lsp` | `pyright-langserver` |
| `rust-analyzer-lsp` | `rust-analyzer` |
| `gopls-lsp` | `gopls` |

### External Integration Plugins‚Äã

Connect Claude to services you already use:

```text
/plugin install github@claude-plugins-official
```

Now Claude can interact with GitHub issues, PRs, and repositories directly.

---

## Installation Scopes‚Äã

When you install a plugin, choose where it applies:

| Scope | Who Uses It | Where It's Stored |
| --- | --- | --- |
| User | Just you, all projects | `~/.claude/` |
| Project | Everyone on this repo | `.claude/settings.json` |
| Local | Just you, this repo only | Local settings |

Recommendation: Start with User scope for personal tools, Project scope for team standards.

---

## Managing Plugins‚Äã

### See What's Installed‚Äã

```text
/plugin
```

Go to the Installed tab.

### Disable Without Uninstalling‚Äã

```text
/plugin disable plugin-name@marketplace-name
```

### Re-enable‚Äã

```text
/plugin enable plugin-name@marketplace-name
```

### Completely Remove‚Äã

```text
/plugin uninstall plugin-name@marketplace-name
```

---

## Adding More Marketplaces‚Äã

The official marketplace is just the start. You can add others:

GitHub repositories:

```text
/plugin marketplace add owner/repo
```

GitLab or other git hosts:

```text
/plugin marketplace add https://gitlab.com/company/plugins.git
```

Local development:

```text
/plugin marketplace add ./my-marketplace
```

### The Official Plugins Repository‚Äã

Anthropic maintains the official plugins repository with verified plugins:

```text
/plugin marketplace add anthropics/claude-plugins-official
```

This gives you access to all officially maintained plugins including Ralph Wiggum (Lesson 17).

---

## When to Use Plugins vs. Build Custom‚Äã

| Situation | Recommendation |
| --- | --- |
| Standard task (git, GitHub, Slack) | Install existing plugin |
| Team-specific workflow | Check marketplace first, then build custom |
| Learning how plugins work | Install examples, study their structure |
| No matching plugin exists | Create custom (see below) |

Rule of thumb: Check the marketplace before building from scratch.

---

## Package and Distribute Your Own Plugin‚Äã

You've installed plugins. Now let's create one from the skills and components you've already built.

Throughout this chapter, you created skills (Lesson 9), configured subagents (Lesson 11), connected MCP servers (Lesson 12), and set up hooks (Lesson 15). Packaging these as a plugin lets you share them with teammates or use them across all your projects.

### Plugin Directory Structure‚Äã

A plugin is a folder with a specific layout:

```text
my-plugin/‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ .claude-plugin/‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ plugin.json          # Required manifest‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ skills/                   # Your SKILL.md files‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ agents/                   # Your subagent definitions‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ hooks/‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ hooks.json           # Your hook configurations‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ .mcp.json                # Your MCP server configs‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ README.md
```

Critical: Components go at the root level, not inside`.claude-plugin/`. The`.claude-plugin/` folder only contains the manifest.

### The Plugin Manifest‚Äã

Every plugin needs a`plugin.json` file inside`.claude-plugin/`:

```json
{  "name": "my-skills",  "description": "My Claude Code skills collection",  "version": "1.0.0",  "author": {    "name": "Your Name"  }}
```

That's the minimum. Four fields. Your plugin is ready to use.

### Try It: Package Skills Lab as a Plugin‚Äã

If you downloaded the Skills Lab in Lesson 7, let's turn it into a plugin:

Step 1: Navigate to your Skills Lab folder:

```bash
cd claude-code-skills-lab
```

Step 2: Create the manifest folder and file:

```bash
mkdir .claude-plugin
```

Step 3: Create`plugin.json`:

```json
{  "name": "skills-lab",  "description": "Practice skills from Panaversity tutorials",  "version": "1.0.0",  "author": {    "name": "Your Name"  }}
```

Step 4: Test your plugin locally:

```text
claude --plugin-dir ./claude-code-skills-lab
```

Step 5: Verify skills appear with namespace:

```text
/skills-lab:internal-comms
```

Your Skills Lab is now a plugin.

### Plugin vs. Marketplace: What's the Difference?‚Äã

| Concept | What It Is | Analogy |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Plugin | A folder with skills, agents, hooks, MCP configs | An app |
| Marketplace | A catalog listing multiple plugins | An app store |

Why have marketplaces at all?

You could share plugins without a marketplace‚Äîjust tell someone to clone your repo and use`--plugin-dir`. But marketplaces provide:

- Updates: Marketplaces can version plugins and notify users of updates
- Organization: Group related plugins (your team's tools, a company's integrations)
- Discovery: Browse what's available instead of knowing exact repo URLs

Can you list your plugin on someone else's marketplace?

Yes. Options include:

1. Your own marketplace: List your plugin plus others you find useful
2. Team/company marketplaces: Ask the maintainer to add your plugin to their`marketplace.json`
3. Official Anthropic marketplace: Submit a PR to get your plugin listed for everyone

Most developers create their own marketplace for personal/team use, then submit polished plugins to the official marketplace for broader distribution.

### Create Your Own Marketplace‚Äã

To share your plugin with others, create a marketplace:

Step 1: Create a`marketplace.json` in your`.claude-plugin/` folder:

```json
{  "name": "my-plugins",  "owner": {    "name": "Your Name"  },  "plugins": [    {      "name": "skills-lab",      "source": "./skills-lab",      "description": "Practice skills collection"    }  ]}
```

Step 2: Push to GitHub (or GitLab, or any git host)

Step 3: Others can now add your marketplace:

```text
/plugin marketplace add your-username/your-repo
```

### Distribution Options‚Äã

| Method | Command | Best For |
| --- | --- | --- |
| GitHub | `/plugin marketplace add owner/repo` | Teams, open source |
| GitLab/Other | `/plugin marketplace add https://...` | Enterprise |
| Local | `/plugin marketplace add ./path` | Testing |

---

### What's Next‚Äã

You can now discover, install, and create plugins‚Äîthe complete lifecycle. Lesson 17 introduces the Ralph Wiggum Loop‚Äîan autonomous iteration pattern where Claude validates and refines its own work. You'll see how to combine everything you've learned (skills, subagents, hooks, and your own plugins) into self-correcting workflows.

---

## Try With AI‚Äã

üîç Explore the Marketplace:

"Run /plugin and show me what's in the Discover tab. What categories of plugins are available? Which ones would be useful for [your work: web development / Python / data analysis]?"

What you're learning: Plugin discovery‚Äîunderstanding what capability extensions exist before building from scratch. The ecosystem often has what you need.

üì¶ Install and Test:

"Help me install the commit-commands plugin. After it's installed, walk me through using /commit-commands:commit to commit a change. What other commands does this plugin provide?"

What you're learning: The full plugin workflow‚Äîfrom installation through verification. Knowing the complete cycle builds confidence with new plugins.

üîå Code Intelligence:

"I write [TypeScript / Python / Rust / Go]. Help me install the LSP plugin for my language. What do I need to install on my system first? After installation, show me how Claude can now jump to definitions and find references."

What you're learning: How plugins add capabilities Claude doesn't have natively‚Äîin this case, language-server-level code understanding.

üîó External Integration:

"I want to connect Claude to [GitHub / Slack / Linear]. Help me install the appropriate plugin. What capabilities does it add? Show me an example of using it."

What you're learning: Platform integration through plugins‚Äîextending Claude's reach to external services without writing custom MCP servers.

‚öñÔ∏è Plugin Decision:

"I need Claude to help with [describe your task]. Should I: (a) install an existing plugin, (b) create a custom skill, (c) just ask Claude directly? Help me decide based on what's available in the marketplace."

What you're learning: The build vs. buy decision for AI capabilities‚Äîwhen to use existing solutions vs. creating custom ones.

üì¶ Package Your Skills:

"Help me package my Skills Lab directory as a plugin. Create the plugin.json manifest, organize my skills into the correct directory structure, and test it with --plugin-dir."

What you're learning: Plugin creation workflow‚Äîturning your existing Claude Code components into shareable, installable packages.

üåê Create Your Marketplace:

"I have a plugin ready. Help me create a marketplace.json file, push it to GitHub, and show me how others can install my plugin."

What you're learning: Plugin distribution‚Äîsharing your work with teammates or the broader community through marketplace catalogs.

## Flashcards Study Aid‚Äã

Try It Now: Install Your First Plugin

- Option 2: Install Directly
- Option 1: Use the UI

What's in the Official Marketplace?

- External Integration Plugins
- Code Intelligence Plugins

Managing Plugins

- Completely Remove
- Re-enable
- Disable Without Uninstalling
- See What's Installed

Adding More Marketplaces

Package and Distribute Your Own Plugin

# Cursor Subagents: The Feature Everyone's Talking About

- Channel: Code & bird
- Length: 13:18
- Views: 4.0K
- Likes: 76
- Published: 2026-03-16T04:32:38-07:00
- Category: Science & Technology
- Keywords: cursor ai, cursor subagents, cursor ai tutorial, cursor ai agents, ai coding tools, ai for developers, ai developer tools, ai software development, ai agents tutorial, ai agents for coding, coding with ai, ai dev workflow, cursor ide tutorial, programming with ai, ai code review, ai, coding, ai coding
- Language: en

## Description

You've seen the LinkedIn posts. "Leveraging AI to 10x my productivity." "Orchestrating a team of agents to ship faster." And if you want to put it on your LinkedIn and actually mean it ‚Äî not just say it ‚Äî watch this video, because I'm going to show you exactly how to do it in Cursor.
We're talking about subagents. Independent, specialised agents that run in parallel under a main agent you control. One researching your codebase, one building the frontend, one running tests, one doing a security audit ‚Äî all at the same time, while you sip your coffee.
By the end of this you'll have three production-ready workflows you can drop into any project today.

## Chapters

- 00:00 Introduction
- 00:57 How Sub Agents Work in Cursor
- 02:02 Creating Custom Sub Agents
- 04:12 Demo: Creating a Security Auditor Agent
- 10:32 Common Mistakes to Avoid

## Transcript

You've seen this LinkedIn post where people claim they are orchestrating a team of AI agents. And you probably think this is something that require a lot of skill and knowledge. But actually, it's very easy to do. And if you want to put it in your LinkedIn profile as well, then stay with me.
Because today I will show you how exactly to do it in cursor. Yes, today we are talking about sub agents, independent specialized agents that run in parallel under the main agent that you control. One researching your code base, one building your front end, one running tests, and one doing a security audit. All at the same time while you're doom scrolling on your phone and sip your coffee. Watch this video and by the end of it, you will have production ready workflows that you can drop into any project. Let's get started. When you kick off a complex task in cursor, you will see sub agents in the agent sidebar.
Hi Amy.
The main agent is orchestrating and sub agents running in parallel. And cursor comes with three built-in sub agents.
First, explore. This sub agent navigates large repos, finds the relevant files, and builds context without polluting the main conversation. Second built-in agent is for bash commands. It allows you to run shell commands in isolation, so the main agent doesn't have to stop and wait for the output. And the third built-in agent is browser. Different tools and MCPs interact with the browser, and the DOM snapshots are really messy and hard to read. So this sub agent filters it down to the relevant results, extracts the important information, and passes it to the main agent. And the coolest thing is that you can also define your own custom sub agents. And that's where it gets really powerful. Custom sub agents are just markdown files. And for Cursor to pick them up, they need to be in a specific folder. If you want to have your agents related to the project, then in the project root, you need to create a folder dot cursor slash agents. And if you want to share your agents between the projects, then you create it in the home directory. So, each markdown file in this folder is a sub agent. You need to follow a specific convention. First, YAML metadata for your agent for Cursor to understand when to call it. And then, instructions for the sub agent. And here you can see an example. So, first, let's break down each metadata field in the YAML formatter section. Name. This is a required field, and Cursor will use it to reference this agent internally.
Then, description. And this is critical.
The main agent reads it and decides what to delegate to this agent. Write it as a job description. Be specific, not vague.
But, don't make it too long to not clog your context. Then, you can define a model for each sub agent. You can set it to inherit, then it will use the same model your main agent uses, or set it to fast, and then Cursor will use the cheapest and fastest model appropriate for the task. You can also hardcode a specific model ID if you know exactly which model you want to use. You can also set a read-only parameter. Set it to true if you want this agent to only read and analyze your code, but don't want it to edit. That's really useful for reviewers and auditors agents. And the last one is background. This parameter set to true makes your agent asynchronous. The parent agent will not wait for it to finish and just continue working. Now, let's try and create our first sub agent for a real-life use case. So, I open my project in Cursor and first thing I'm going to do is to create a directory where our sub agents will live. The directory called .cursor and inside it I will also create another one called agents. And now, let's create our first agent.
The name of this agent will be a security auditor. So, let's go and just create it.
Security auditor.md.
Once I created it, you can see that it didn't just open an MD file like a normal document. It opened it with this little UI on the top and we have a name here. It took it from the file name, that's fine.
It has a model and we can tell it, for example, to use different model than I usually run. I usually run the Claude Sonnet 4.6, but for security audit, I want to run a more advanced model. And next, we need to add a description, and that's a very important part because that's how Cursor understands when to call this agent. And I'll just copy-paste it, and you can see it appeared here as well.
This agent reviews code for security vulnerabilities, use when new API endpoints, authentication logic, database database queries, or user input handling are added. Next, we need to actually describe what this agent is doing. And again, I'm just going to copy-paste it because we don't have much time to type it in. Or you can even ask an AI to create you this auditor. I guess it will create something similar. Uh so, what this sub-agent will do as a senior application security engineer, it will review the code and check for the following uh issues. And um for every finding, we'll report uh the file and line of code with the issue, uh severity, the attack vector, and specific code fix. Um so, uh what else can we do here? We also can tell it to run in the background, and it will add this parameter is background true.
And we can make it read-only because we don't want our auditor to change code.
We only want it to read, analyze, and report back about the issues. Okay, so now let's um save it, and let's try to use it. Okay, now let's see our sub-agent in action. Just on a side note, I just realized that I don't have a Cursor Pro subscription anymore. I canceled it because I got another one for my work. And uh that's why I changed the model to auto here because in free Cursor tier, you cannot use um models of your choice. And unfortunately, they also removed an ability to add an API key. So, there's only auto model, but that's fine. It will work for the demo anyway.
So, let's test it. And what I'm going to do, I am going to tell Cursor to create a form uh for login and password and I also tell it to store login and password in the local storage of the browser.
This is obviously not something you want to do in a real life. This is very insecure.
Uh but we want to trigger our security auditor sub agent and see if it will work.
Anyway, let's do it. Create a login form for username and password and save the user input in the local storage of the browser.
Uh call security auditor sub agent to verify the code and I will submit and let's see what will happen. And you can see that it also uses different skills. It uses my superpowers plugin that kind of helps to write better code but it consumes a lot more tokens than just normal requests. So this is kind of a trade-off but anyway. So it did something. Uh and now you can see that it called our sub agent and if you click on it, you can see what's going on inside of this sub agent in its own chat in its own context and you can see that the security auditor completed and it adjusted our code uh to the requirements to be safe.
Uh so basically it saved only username in the local storage avoiding the insecure insecure pattern of storing plain text password.
Uh so this worked and it also gave me sub-agent result. And interestingly, it applied the fix even though the sub-agent has no all right permissions.
So, the main agent acted according the sub-agent findings and fixed its code. That's quite interesting. But anyway, you can see it works and it is quite helpful.
And if by any reason your sub-agent didn't get called automatically, you can also still do it manually using this {slash} comments. Just start typing the name and it will pop up here so you can select it. It will get highlighted. Then you just type whatever you want it to do and it will work. So, that's our demo.
You can see how useful it is and how to set up it in your own code. And the last thing I want to mention is some mistakes that you want to avoid while using sub-agents. And the first mistake, do not create too many sub-agents. Yes, when you see how powerful they can be, it is kind of tempting to go and create 20 sub-agents for any small use case.
But if you do create too many agents, then your workflow can easily become a mess and your main agent will spend more time just deciding to which agent to delegate the tasks instead of actually performing them. So, start with two or three agents and see how it goes.
Another very common mistake is using vague names and descriptions for your agents. Main agent uses descriptions to understand which tasks it can delegate to the agents. And if your description is too vague, then the main agent can try to delegate everything to this sub agent or nothing. So, try to specific So, try to be as specific as possible to make this automated delegation work. And the third mistake, try to avoid running multiple agents on the same files.
Yes, Cursor uses Git work trees to work on the files, so they work on a separate copies of the code base. But, when you integrate the output, merge conflicts can happen if multiple agents touch the same files. So, before creating and running sub agents, think about the ownership. If two features need to touch the same files, it's better to run them sequentially. And that's why you also can see the common pattern of separating the ownership when people create separate agents for implementing, separate agents for testing, and for reviewing the code. Parallel only truly independent tasks. And then your workflow will stay simple and clean. So, that's it for today, and I hope this video was useful for you. Try to run your sub agents, and let me know if it works for you. If you don't know where to start, maybe try to create one read-only sub agent and see if you can make it run comfortably alongside your main agent. Please let me know in the comments below. What do you think about sub agents? Do you use them in your everyday work? And how it is going for you? And in the next video, I will show you even more cool Cursor features. So, stay tuned, like this video, and subscribe for my channel. And see you in the next video.

results.text:
Agent plugins in VS Code (Preview)

üìº Rewatch VS Code Live at MS Build 2026

# Agent plugins in VS Code (Preview)

Agent plugins are prepackaged bundles of agent customizations that you can discover and install from plugin marketplaces in Visual Studio Code. A single plugin can provide any combination of slash commands, agent skills, custom agents, hooks, and MCP servers.

Plugins work alongside your locally defined customizations. When you install a plugin, its commands, skills, agents, hooks, and MCP servers appear in chat.

Note

Enable or disable support for agent plugins with the chat.plugins.enabled Open in VS Code Open in VS Code Insiders setting.

## What plugins provide

An agent plugin can bundle one or more of the following customization types:

- Slash commands: additional commands you can invoke with`/` in chat
- Skills: agent skills with instructions, scripts, and resources that load on-demand
- Agents: custom agents with specialized personas and tool configurations
- Hooks: hooks that execute shell commands at agent lifecycle points
- MCP servers: MCP servers for external tool integrations

For example, a testing plugin might include a`test-runner` skill with scripts, a`test-reviewer` agent with read-only tools, and an MCP server for a test reporting dashboard. The plugin directory structure looks like this:

Text

```
my-testing-plugin/
  plugin.json              # Plugin metadata and configuration
  skills/
    test-runner/
      SKILL.md             # Testing skill instructions
      run-tests.sh         # Supporting script
  agents/
    test-reviewer.agent.md # Code review agent
  hooks/
    hooks.json             # Hook configuration
  scripts/
    validate-tests.sh      # Hook script
  .mcp.json                # MCP server definitions

```

Once installed, plugin-provided customizations appear alongside your locally defined ones. For example, skills from a plugin show up in the Configure Skills menu, and MCP servers from a plugin appear in the MCP server list.

Caution

Plugins can include hooks and MCP servers that run code on your machine. Review the plugin contents and publisher before installing, especially for plugins from community marketplaces.

## Plugin metadata (plugin.json)

Every plugin requires a`plugin.json` manifest file at its root. This file defines the plugin's identity and tells VS Code where to find its components.

### Required field

Expand table

| Field | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `name` | string | Kebab-case plugin name. Only lowercase letters, numbers, and hyphens are allowed. Maximum 64 characters. Do not use slashes, colons, or namespace prefixes (for example,`my-plugin` is valid but`myorg/my-plugin` is not). Invalid names cause the plugin to silently fail to load. |

### Optional fields

Expand table

| Field | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `description` | string | Brief description of the plugin. Maximum 1024 characters. |
| `version` | string | Semantic version (for example,`1.0.0`). When a plugin is listed in a marketplace, version can appear in both`plugin.json` and the`marketplace.json` plugin entry. Bump the version in`plugin.json` when you publish changes. |
| `author` | object | Author information with`name`(required),`email`, and`url` fields. |
| `skills` | string or string[] | Path(s) to skill directories. Defaults to`skills/`. |
| `agents` | string or string[] | Path(s) to agent directories. Defaults to`agents/`. |
| `hooks` | string or object | Path to a hooks config file or an inline hooks object. |
| `mcpServers` | string or object | Path to an MCP config file (for example,`.mcp.json`) or inline server definitions. |

For the full field reference, see the GitHub Copilot CLI plugin reference.

### Example plugin.json

JSON

```
{
  "name": "my-dev-tools",
  "description": "React development utilities",
  "version": "1.2.0",
  "author": {
    "name": "Jane Doe"
  },
  "skills": "skills/",
  "agents": "agents/",
  "hooks": "hooks.json",
  "mcpServers": ".mcp.json"
}

```

## Plugin formats

VS Code auto-detects the plugin format by checking for format-specific manifest paths. Copilot format is used as the default when no other format markers are found.

Expand table

| Plugin format | Plugin file path(s) |
| --- | --- |
| Claude | `.claude-plugin/plugin.json` |
| OpenPlugin | `.plugin/plugin.json` |

### Plugin environment variables

Some plugin formats provide a root token that you can use in hook commands and MCP server configurations to reference files within the plugin directory. VS Code expands the token at runtime and also sets it as an environment variable in the hook or server process.

Expand table

| Plugin format | Plugin root |
| --- | --- |
| Claude | `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}` |
| Copilot | (Not defined) |
| OpenPlugin | `${PLUGIN_ROOT}` |

## Hooks in plugins

Plugins can include hooks that run shell commands at agent lifecycle points. Plugin hooks work alongside your workspace and user-level hooks. When a plugin is enabled, its hooks fire in addition to any other hooks configured for the same event.

### Hook file location

The hook file location depends on the plugin format:

Expand table

| Plugin format | Hook file path |
| --- | --- |
| Claude | `hooks/hooks.json` |
| Copilot | `hooks.json`(at the plugin root) |

VS Code auto-detects the plugin format and discovers the hook file automatically.

Text

```
my-plugin/
  hooks/
    hooks.json           # Hook configuration (Claude format)
  scripts/
    format.sh            # Hook script referenced by hooks.json

```

### Hook configuration format

Plugin hooks use the same base format as workspace hooks. VS Code parses Claude Code hook configuration, including matcher syntax. Currently, VS Code ignores matcher values, so hooks run on every matching event.

Flat format (same as workspace hooks):

JSON

```
{
  "hooks": {
    "PostToolUse": [
      {
        "type": "command",
        "command": "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/format.sh"
      }
    ]
  }
}

```

Matcher format (Claude compatibility syntax):

JSON

```
{
  "hooks": {
    "PostToolUse": [
      {
        "matcher": "Write|Edit",
        "hooks": [
          {
            "type": "command",
            "command": "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/format.sh"
          }
        ]
      }
    ]
  }
}

```

VS Code parses the`matcher` field for compatibility with Claude Code, but currently ignores matcher values. If you need to filter hook behavior in VS Code, check the event input inside the hook script.

### Reference plugin paths in hook commands

For Claude-format plugins, use the`${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}` token in hook commands to reference scripts and files within the plugin directory. VS Code expands this token to the plugin's absolute path at runtime and also sets a`CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT` environment variable for the hook process. Inside your script, access this as`$CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT`(or`%CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT%` on Windows).

This is important because plugins are installed to a location outside your workspace, so you cannot use relative paths.

JSON

```
{
  "hooks": {
    "PreToolUse": [
      {
        "type": "command",
        "command": "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/validate-tool.sh"
      }
    ]
  }
}

```

### Supported hook events

Plugin hooks support the same lifecycle events as workspace hooks:`SessionStart`,`UserPromptSubmit`,`PreToolUse`,`PostToolUse`,`PreCompact`,`SubagentStart`,`SubagentStop`, and`Stop`. See Hook lifecycle events for details on each event.

### How plugin hooks interact with other hooks

Plugin hooks run alongside workspace-level and user-level hooks. When multiple hooks target the same event, all of them execute. For`PreToolUse` hooks, the most restrictive permission decision across all hooks wins:`deny` overrides`ask`, which overrides`allow`.

Disabling a plugin also disables its hooks. You can enable or disable plugins globally or for a specific workspace from the Extensions view.

## MCP servers in plugins

Plugins can bundle MCP servers to provide agents with additional tools and data sources. Plugin MCP servers start automatically when the plugin is enabled and stop when the plugin is disabled.

### MCP configuration file

Place MCP server definitions in`.mcp.json` at the plugin root. VS Code discovers this file automatically when it loads the plugin.

Text

```
my-plugin/
  .mcp.json              # MCP server definitions
  servers/
    db-server             # Server executable
  config.json             # Server configuration

```

### MCP configuration format

Plugin MCP servers are defined in a top-level`mcpServers` object. Each server entry specifies a command, arguments, and optional environment variables:

JSON

```
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "plugin-database": {
      "command": "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/servers/db-server",
      "args": ["--config", "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/config.json"],
      "env": {
        "DB_PATH": "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/data"
      }
    },
    "plugin-api": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["@company/mcp-server", "--plugin-mode"],
      "cwd": "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}"
    }
  }
}

```

Note

The top-level key is`mcpServers`(not`servers` as in the workspace`mcp.json`).

### Reference plugin paths in server configuration

For Claude-format plugins, use the`${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}` token in MCP server fields to reference executables and files within the plugin directory. VS Code expands this token in the following fields:

- `command`: the executable path
- `args`: command-line arguments
- `cwd`: working directory
- `env`: environment variable values
- `envFile`: path to an environment file
- `url`: for HTTP-based MCP servers
- `headers`: HTTP header values

VS Code also injects a`CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT` environment variable into the server process, so server code can access the plugin path at runtime.

### How plugin MCP servers interact with other servers

Plugin MCP servers appear alongside workspace and user-level MCP servers. You can manage them through the same tools:

- Select Configure Tools in the Chat view to see tools from all MCP servers, including plugin servers.
- Run MCP: List Servers from the Command Palette to view plugin servers alongside other servers.

Plugin MCP servers are implicitly trusted when you install the plugin. Unlike workspace MCP servers, they do not show a separate trust prompt at startup.

Disabling a plugin stops its MCP servers. Tools provided by the stopped servers are no longer available in chat.

## Discover and install plugins

VS Code provides a dedicated view in the Extensions sidebar to browse and manage agent plugins.

### Browse available plugins

Open the Extensions view (Ctrl+Shift+X) and enter`@agentPlugins` in the search field.

Alternatively, select the More Actions (three dots) icon in the Extensions sidebar and choose Views > Agent Plugins.

Browse the list of available plugins from your configured marketplaces.

Select Install to install a plugin in your user profile.

The first time you install a plugin from a new marketplace, VS Code shows a trust prompt. Review the marketplace source before confirming.

### Install a plugin from source

You can install a plugin directly from a Git repository URL, without adding a full marketplace first.

- Run Chat: Install Plugin From Source from the Command Palette.
- Alternatively, select the + button on the Plugins page of the Agent Customizations editor.

Enter a Git repository URL (for example,`https://github.com/rwoll/markdown-review`) and VS Code clones and installs the plugin.

### Plugins installed by GitHub Copilot CLI

VS Code automatically discovers plugins that you install with the GitHub Copilot CLI to enable you to use them also in VS Code. Plugins from`~/.copilot/installed-plugins/` appear in the Agent Plugins - Installed view alongside plugins you installed from a marketplace or from source.

The CLI stores plugins under`~/.copilot/installed-plugins/ / /`. Plugins installed directly from a Git URL (rather than from a marketplace) live under the`_direct` bucket, for example`~/.copilot/installed-plugins/_direct/github--moda-linter--copilot-plugin/`.

### View installed plugins

The Agent Plugins - Installed view in the Extensions view shows the plugins you have installed. From this view, you can enable, disable, or uninstall plugins.

You can also manage installed plugins from the Chat view by selecting the gear icon > Plugins.

### Enable or disable plugins

You can enable or disable a plugin globally or for a specific workspace:

- Use the context menu on a plugin in the Agent Plugins - Installed section of the Extensions view.
- Use the Agent Customizations editor to toggle a plugin's enabled state.

The enable/disable state is stored separately from the plugin configuration, so it does not affect shared workspace settings.

When a plugin is disabled, its skills, agents, hooks, MCP servers, and slash commands are no longer available. For example, skills from a disabled plugin do not appear in Chat: Configure Skills. Disabled plugins appear with a dimmed style in the Agent Customizations editor and Extensions view.

### Uninstall plugins

To remove a plugin, right-click it in the Agent Plugins - Installed view and select Uninstall. Plugins installed from an external source (such as npm, PyPI, or an external Git repository) are removed from disk. Plugins that are inlined in a marketplace repository remain on disk but are no longer active.

## Configure plugin marketplaces

By default, VS Code discovers plugins from the copilot-plugins and awesome-copilot. You can add additional marketplaces with the chat.plugins.marketplaces Open in VS Code Open in VS Code Insiders setting.

Marketplaces are Git repositories that contain plugin definitions. You can reference them in several formats:

- Shorthand:`owner/repo` for public GitHub repositories. For example,`anthropics/claude-code`.
- HTTPS git remote: a full URL ending in`.git`. For example,`https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code.git`.
- SCP-style git remote: SSH-style references. For example,`git@github.com:anthropics/claude-code.git`.
- file URI: a`file:///` path to a marketplace repository already cloned on disk.

Private repositories are also supported. If a public lookup fails, VS Code falls back to cloning the repository directly.

Marketplace plugins can also reference external package sources such as npm or PyPI packages. For the full marketplace plugin schema, see the Claude Code plugin marketplace documentation.

JSON

```
// settings.json
"chat.plugins.marketplaces": [
    "anthropics/claude-code"
]

```

Note

Enterprise admins can centrally control which plugins and marketplaces are available to developers. For more information, see Manage agent plugins and marketplaces.

## Use local plugins

If you manually clone or download a plugin, you can register it with the chat.pluginLocations Open in VS Code Open in VS Code Insiders setting. This setting maps local plugin directory paths to an enabled or disabled state.

JSON

```
// settings.json
"chat.pluginLocations": {
    "/path/to/my-plugin": true,
    "/path/to/another-plugin": false
}

```

Set the value to`true` to enable the plugin, or`false` to keep it registered but disabled.

## Update plugins

VS Code checks for plugin updates when you run Extensions: Check for Extension Updates from the Command Palette, or automatically every 24 hours when extensions.autoUpdate Open in VS Code Open in VS Code Insiders is enabled.

Updating pulls down changes from cloned marketplace repositories and checks for new versions of externally sourced plugins.

Plugins sourced from npm or PyPI never update automatically. Instead, they show an Update button in the Extensions view. Selecting the button prompts you to confirm before running the install command. If an update is found during a background check, no action is taken until you explicitly select Update.

## Workspace plugin recommendations

Projects can recommend plugins for team members by configuring plugin settings in the workspace settings (`.claude/settings.json` or`.github/copilot/settings.json`).

VS Code shows a notification the first time a chat message is sent. You can view the recommended plugins by opening the Extensions view and filtering by`@agentPlugins @recommended`.

Specify the following fields in the settings file to configure workspace plugin recommendations:

`extraKnownMarketplaces`: registers additional marketplaces for the project. These marketplaces appear when you search`@agentPlugins` in the Extensions view.

`enabledPlugins`: lists plugins that should be enabled by default.

JSON

```
{
  "extraKnownMarketplaces": {
    "company-tools": {
      "source": {
        "source": "github",
        "repo": "your-org/plugin-marketplace"
      }
    }
  },
  "enabledPlugins": {
    "code-formatter@company-tools": true
  }
}

```

## Cross-tool compatibility

The plugin format is shared between VS Code, GitHub Copilot CLI, and Claude Code. A single plugin repository can work across all three tools.

VS Code auto-detects the plugin format by looking for`plugin.json` in multiple locations, checked in this order:

1. `.plugin/plugin.json`
2. `plugin.json`(at the plugin root)
3. `.github/plugin/plugin.json`
4. `.claude-plugin/plugin.json`

If you author plugins for multiple tools, you can place`plugin.json` at the root and use symlinks or copies in the format-specific directories. Keep the`name` field identical across all copies to avoid conflicts.

Key differences to be aware of across tools:

- Hook file location: Claude-format plugins expect hooks in`hooks/hooks.json`, while Copilot-format plugins use`hooks.json` at the root. VS Code detects the format automatically.
- Plugin root token: Claude-format plugins use`${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}` to reference files within the plugin directory. This token is not available in Copilot-format plugins.
- Skill naming: all tools require plain kebab-case names in`SKILL.md`. Namespace prefixes (like`myorg/skillname`) cause silent load failures.

For tool-specific details, see the GitHub Copilot CLI plugin reference and the Claude Code plugin marketplace documentation.

## Troubleshooting

### Plugin does not appear after installation

- Confirm that agent plugins are enabled: check that chat.plugins.enabled Open in VS Code Open in VS Code Insiders is set to`true`.
- Verify the plugin's`name` field in`plugin.json` uses only lowercase letters, numbers, and hyphens. Slashes, colons, or other special characters cause the plugin to silently fail to load.
- Check that`plugin.json` is in a recognized location (see Cross-tool compatibility).

### Skills from a plugin do not load

- Open the`SKILL.md` file and check the`name` field in the YAML frontmatter. The name must be plain kebab-case without namespace prefixes (for example,`test-runner`, not`myorg/test-runner`). Invalid names cause the skill to be silently skipped.
- Make sure the skill directory name matches the`name` field in the`SKILL.md` frontmatter.

### Plugin version does not update

- Bump the`version` field in`plugin.json`(and in the`marketplace.json` plugin entry, if applicable) before pushing changes.
- Run Extensions: Check for Extension Updates from the Command Palette to trigger an update check.

### Installation fails with 'destination path already exists'

This can happen when a previous install left cached data. Delete the cached plugin directory and retry:

- macOS:`~/Library/Application Support/Code/agentPlugins/github.com/{org}/{repo}`
- Linux:`~/.config/Code/agentPlugins/github.com/{org}/{repo}`
- Windows:`%APPDATA%\Code\agentPlugins\github.com\{org}\{repo}`
